title: Andrew J. Liang
text: I am a pre-Political Science major at UCSB, studying urban and judicial politics. I also enjoy learning about statistics and data visualization. Outside of school, I enjoy playing video games, competing in pickleball tournaments and leagues, photography, following the adventures (and misadventures) of the Oakland Athletics, and using dark mode for every application whenever possible.

title: About
text:
I am a first-year Political Science student at UCSB, studying urban and judicial politics. Some of my favorite classes taken so far include:

HIST 142AL - American Legal and Constitutional History
POL S 106AD - American Political Development, and
ENV S 193DS - Statistics for Environmental Studies

All of these courses touch upon my research interests, and my instructors have all been influential towards the standards I set for my own work.
As a kid, I had multiple interests:

riding the Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) system (and maybe becoming a train operator for it someday),
computers,
baseball, and
becoming the President of the United States.

I gave up the first and third options pretty quickly, because I realized that (1) constantly asking your parents to take you on weekly rides on BART wasn’t an economically or academically smart choice when I turned 5, and (2) becoming the President of the United States opens you to new challenges that I thought I would not take well. I gave up studying computers after I realized I barely understood Patterson and Hennessy (1994). Finally, my fastball in fifth grade was clocked in at under 40 miles per hour. All of

title: Photography
text:
I’ve also recently gotten into photography thanks to a high school digital photography course I took in my senior year. One of the unfortunate consequences of moving to UCSB is that I no longer have my own camera to use; nevertheless, Santa Barbara as a whole is extremely photogenic and I plan to take more pictures once I am able to obtain a new camera in the future.
Here are some pictures I took as part of my digital photography course:

title: Data Visualization
text:
My favorite assignments in ENV S 193DS all included problems on data visualization. I have especially become indebted to several packages designed by R’s Hadley Wickham, including ggplot2 and ggmap.
Outside of ENV S 193DS, I have also learned to use the ggmap and sf packages to create informative maps. This knowledge was especially useful for my assignments in POL S 162 - Urban Government and Politics, where several projects involving data collection and analysis were assigned.



This map shows the boundaries and area for two Northern California cities, paired in a project investigating the success of their Housing Element submissions to the California Department of Housing and Community Development. The purpose of this map was to illustrate the difference in size between the two cities, which led to different approaches towards plans for new housing units for each city. To create this map, I used several spatial packages, including sf, ggmap, and gridExtra. Shapefiles for the city boundaries and areas were provided under an ArcGIS dataset provided by the California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection. In my final report, I noted that Saratoga’s planned housing development was centered around the northeastern portion of the city, near the Quito Village area. This, in contrast with Piedmont’s decision to spread its planned housing units along the city, resulted in HCD’s ruling that Saratoga’s housing element was non-compliant with state law, even when it had more area for housing development than Piedmont.

title: About
text:
I was born in Hayward, California, but have lived in neighboring Fremont for much of my life. As a kid, I had multiple interests:

riding the Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) system (and maybe becoming a train operator for it someday),
computers,
baseball, and
becoming the President of the United States.

I gave up the first and third options pretty quickly, because I realized that (1) constantly asking your parents to take you on weekly rides on BART wasn’t an economically or academically smart choice when I turned 5, and (2) becoming the President of the United States opens you to new challenges that I thought I would not take well. I gave up studying computers after I realized I barely understood (Patterson and Hennessy 1994). Finally, my fastball in fifth grade was clocked in at under 40 miles per hour. Thus, all of those interests eventually faded from view.
